text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Traumatic brain injury (historical, mild with residual executive dysfunction)/osteopenia/low mood under primary care review/smoker (reduced to five per day)/reports sleep fragmentation and intermittent snoring under community sleep assessment. 

Medication: 
- Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily.  
- Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily.  
- Clobazam 10 mg at night as required for brief clusters (patient-initiated, within agreed ceiling).  
- Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily, Omeprazole 20 mg daily, Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly.  

I reviewed Bill in clinic today, accompanied by his sister who provides informal support. Since the last review he has been engaging with community physiotherapy for balance confidence and has resumed part-time volunteering at a local charity shop, which he finds helpful for routine. He reports improved hydration and more regular meals. Mood remains variable but he denies self-harm thoughts; he is willing to re-engage with talking therapy if offered locally. 

Seizures: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported. He and his sister note occasional cluster patterns linked to sleep loss, which aligns with his established triggers. He keeps a home diary; entries are broadly consistent with the above, and he carries a personalised plan documented elsewhere that outlines early measures during clusters and when to seek help. He has not required emergency department attendance in the interval. No new injuries reported. 

Examination: Weight 78 kg (BMI 24.9). BP 128/76, pulse 72 regular. Neurological examination unchanged: full power, tone normal, gait cautious but non-ataxic; Romberg negative. No tongue biting scars, no shoulder tenderness. 

Investigations: Levetiracetam and lamotrigine levels previously within expected clinical ranges (April 2025). Routine bloods in August 2025 normal including LFTs, U&Es, vitamin D 62 nmol/L. No recent neuroimaging indicated; last MRI (2023) unchanged. 

Discussion: He identifies sleep loss as a consistent precipitant for clusters and is using phone reminders for lights-out and morning wake times. He has a pending community sleep study to evaluate possible obstructive sleep apnoea given snoring and non-restorative sleep. He confirms understanding of his personalised plan and has shared a copy with his sister and the charity shop supervisor. Driving remains not applicable. No safeguarding concerns. 

Plan: 
- Continue current anti-seizure medication regimen without change.  
- Reinforce adherence and sleep hygiene; he will continue using the personalised cluster-response plan already documented elsewhere.  
- Community sleep study to proceed as scheduled; we will review results when available.  
- Repeat routine bloods (U&Es, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D) via GP in 6 months or sooner if clinically indicated.  
- Provide a brief summary for his employer regarding agreed adjustments (flexible breaks, safe recovery space).  
- Follow-up in neurology clinic in 6 months, with earlier review if patterns change or injuries occur.  

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states multiple seizure types and frequencies: "one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month," "daily absences," and "focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks," plus drop attacks without quantified frequency. Applying the rule to select the highest frequency, "daily absences" represents the most frequent occurrence, i.e., seizures happening every day. Therefore, the normalized label is 1 per day. The note "Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported" refers only to the interval since the visit and does not establish 6 months seizure freedom, and elsewhere daily absences are clearly ongoing.
reference: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported
